transcript.transcript: My name is Thad Webster. I'm a participant in the America 250 Idaho Oral History Project. Today is the 9th of February, 2026. I'm talking with John Bertram, City Planner, about his memories and experiences of historical preservation projects in Boise. This oral history is conducted with the support of the Idaho Historical Society and TheirStory as part of the America 250 and Idaho commemoration. This oral history project supports the commemorations' mission to advance the ideals of the American Revolution - equality, liberty and justice - through meaningful community engagement. This project seeks to recognize, appreciate, and commemorate Idaho's journey in America's legacy. First, John, I want to say thank you so much for your time today. This interview will allow you to reflect on and share your memories and experiences, while finding and creating so many of Boise's historical moments and places. I hope to gain insights into how the City of Boise has recognized significant historical moments, and the roles you played. We will also discuss parts of Boise's train history in particular. I'll begin the interview with some general questions to start the conversation, and we can get into more specific topics from there during the interview. I may adjust the order of some of the questions to allow for a natural flow of topics, and I might ask unscripted follow up questions to clarify your answers. Before we jump into the questions, do you give me permission to record this interview? I do. Can I have you state and spell your first and last name, please? John Bertram j o h n b e r t r a m. Thank you very much. First question I'm going to ask you is how did you choose your profession of city planner? My father was a professor and a good friend of his, another professor, was at University of Washington named, uh, uh, and, uh. So, he, encouraged me to look into city planning, I'm glad I did. I've had a great career in it. Well, good. How did you choose Boise to live and work in? Well, interesting story. I didn't choose it. I joined Vista -- Volunteers in Service to America -- in 1969. I might have had, might have been drafted, otherwise. I didn't think I wanted to go to Vietnam. And Vista seemed like a a way to learn and learn about our country. And, so I went back to New York and was trained for two weeks, and, I signed up for three eastern cities, hoping that's where I'd be because I'd never lived on the East Coast. I had an interesting at this time I in my life, I, I thought I'd in the day I was a really good swimmer in high school. I teach African American kids how to swim. And, uh, in the evening, I was just learning how to play the guitar. Well, uh, it turns out that, uh, they chose five of us to go to to go to Idaho. And I wasn't expecting to come back to the northwest. And I asked if I could be in the largest city and that was Boise. So I ended up in Boise as a Vista volunteer. There was one other one, a fellow named Vic Caine, with me, and we ended up living with a family for a couple of weeks. Uh, Norm Bishop, who had the Volkswagen dealership, was very good to us. And, uh, we ended up, uh, finding a little house down in the River Street neighborhood, uh, for $50 a month, and we're there for over a year. Well, River Street, of course, the people in Boise know that as, uh, quite a historical district. Did you know you were going to be landing in in a historical place? Well, we knew we were there to fight poverty. So no surprise that we'd be in an area that had a lot of low income people and mixed race. Um, we didn't have many wealthy people in the River Street neighborhood. Yeah. Yeah. Homeowners who lived outside of the area, who owned homes and rented them to people. And a few people were starting to buy their own homes, but, uh, mostly renters. Yeah. Yeah. So what were some of the first projects you got involved in there? Well, um, we, um, we started to do, um, house painting projects. Um, I found that Boise was very generous. If we went to a paint store and told them we were trying to help the neighborhood. Um, they were they were very good to us. And they would, uh, give us some brushes and some thinner and give us some paint. And, uh, so we started, uh, painting houses. Now, painting houses takes a lot of work. We ended up recruiting some people from Boise State, same age group, and that gave us enough manpower. I think we ended up painting about, uh, about 4 or 5 houses, um, in, in River Street. Um. So, you know, a lot of prep work, um, and, uh, and then I remember getting up on the roof, uh, for 14th and River, um, and, uh, painting the there was a metal, uh, kind of, uh, oh point to it. And I had it, I had I had been around tree forts a lot as a kid. We had tree forts up pretty high. So it didn't bother me to be up there. I mean, we had ladders, but probably in this day and age you wouldn't do it that way. Oh. So what year was this when you were first starting on Vista Projects there in Boise. 1969 through 1970. And that that was the length of your volunteer time with Vista. Right. Just a year's length. And then what did you transition to after Vista? Well, um, I, uh, ended up buying a house down in the River Street neighborhood, uh, on South 14th Street for less than $10,000. And, uh, you know, my dad had always been pretty good at fixing things up, and the house was had structurally was okay. Um, and, uh, so I started to, um, I was good at making repairs and painting and things like that. I remember getting up on the roof, to my surprise, uh, one of my feet started to go through the shingles in a rotten spot, so. Oh. The house hadn't had any any maintenance for a long time. Um, so I, uh, I'm pretty good at doing a lot of things I had done as a kid. Some my dad was the same way. He and his brother helped my grandmother fix up their house, and I remember, you know, doing various things, painting a little bit and helping and driving some nails and things like that. So I had some experience in that. Pretty handy. Yeah. And, uh, so I started to fix that house up and I still have that house. My daughter in law lives in that house on South 14th Street. Yeah, yeah. And then, uh, what was your first Boise City Urban planning. City planning effort? Um. Well, we we started, I, I, I started a firm called Plan Makers. I came up with that word plan makers. Yeah. And, uh, um, you know, I haven't really have that list in front of me, but, uh, we got jobs. Uh, we would we would send an application in or we'd see an opportunity. And I ended up I think I have a list here of the places that we we worked in, but. Were there any parks or. Not? Not really. Parks areas. No separate area. But, uh, I guess I got started mostly. My first real job was, uh, going to work for a guy named Winston Moore who owned property in the eighth Street warehouse area. And I should just a little background on me. My professors were interested in historic preservation in Seattle, and, uh, I actually worked, I think. What did they have me do? Make copies, run errands, you know, you know, the young kid in the office. But I was around University of Washington professors and, uh, got to see what what they were doing. So, um, um, basically, I, you know, I did a whole I basically would learn I learned how to kind of create these nice reports about a community. And we didn't really do a report on, well, we did do a River Street design center. Um, and uh, we got some money for that. That money was always, uh, uncertain. It was only for a year, maybe two years. Yeah, yeah. Um, so, um, but I ended up doing work at, um, I went because I worked with these professors in historic areas I thought maybe I could buy some old warehouse. So I didn't have any money in the warehouse district. And I went to a fellow named Winston Moore, who owned 2 or 3 of those buildings on eighth Street. Yeah. And he said, well, I'm not going to sell, but I'll hire you. So that's how it. Yeah, I went to work for Winston and, uh, basically we were, um, more bookkeepers keeping track of money that was coming in and, and what was happening. And I realized that that I really wasn't very much in charge of, of, you know, I, I he. I had assignments and I got off track of being a city planner or preservationist. And so I didn't last too long with Winston Moore. I, I did some things for him. But one of my maybe one of the, the first jobs I had was I. So I went to Joan Carley and she owned quite a few buildings in the old Boise area. And Joan really appreciated having someone who had some interest in history and wanted to get things done. So, uh, we did a, we did a basically a plan for old Boise. What would that consist of? It would be some maps. It'd be some boundaries. It'd be a list of the buildings. It'd be a list of some of the opportunities of what should be done to those to those buildings. We weren't fixing the buildings, but we were setting up a plan for that. Yeah. So. Yeah. Uh, and eventually we would, uh, you know, I would go to the archives of the Idaho State Historical Society to the library and look up any information I could find on any one of these buildings and put together a background, uh, to to build off of. Oh, cool. So, uh, we went from old Boise, um, eighth Street to old Boise, uh, did the same thing up in Hyde Park, which we know is a fun area. I basically did the same thing. Um, collected information, um, about it. Worked with the owners. Uh, we ended up doing some painting with, uh, I recruited a couple of my buddies to do some painting on a couple of the storefronts in Hyde Park. Um, as I say, we were not professional painters. We were decent. You know, we didn't spill a lot of paint. Um, but then one of the next jobs and one of the best ones I had was up at Idaho City. Normally that, you know, they did. We didn't just, uh, find these things. There'd be a request for proposal kind of. Thing. From a city saying and because of this other work. So Idaho City was the same thing. I mean, it's got a great history and trying to understand Idaho's history of history. So mining history. Yeah. So, uh, we would put together a plan. I think we spent a lot of time looking at how can we improve the boardwalks to walk up and down the street and what how would you how would you build them to hold up over time? Because you've got snow and water and things like that, and you've got drainage issues like, like that. And we didn't do too much paint up cleanup as far as buildings. We basically would be encouraging the owner to do that kind of thing and trying to find them the paint or materials or things like that. If we could get, you know, the open. Um, there was a couple people I remember their names now that were influential down in Boise that were young and appreciated having someone care about they they wanted to buy an old building, but they didn't want to be. They wanted others to be fixing up, you know, they. Wanted. Things moving together as a group, not just a few. Um, so, um, so Idaho City was a good place, you know, and I. You mentioned, uh, you know, in the process there of recounting several projects, getting into, uh, probably some interface with city groups. Um, did you get to know and work with, like, certain Boise planning groups or Boise preservation? Byron Johnson comes into mind of fella from up in that area. So we get to know some fairly interesting people who were movers and shakers in their own right. Um, Ron Terwilliger had bought a property up there. Yeah. So we got to know people like this. And, uh, um, so I think what we were trying to do was get a lot of people working on their buildings. You know, we were trying to figure out and we ended up getting some. I think I was also pretty good at writing grants. So working with the city, we would get like 10 or $15,000 for one of those buildings that the city owned. They have an old church up there. They turned into City Hall, still the City Hall in Idaho City. So, uh, getting a, you know, 15, $20,000 to basically end up a little bit in plumbing, a little bit in paint, a little bit in repair. Uh, we weren't doing those things, but we were we were focused on helping them get some of those things done. Okay, okay. Well, we we got to meet city council members, and, uh, we were there, young kid. Uh, I mean, it wasn't that young, but, you know, uh, dedicated, I guess I would say. Yeah, having some experience at having other successes. Uh, and they really appreciated having someone who cared about history and wanted to make repairs. And the big picture was we wanted to make Idaho City more attractive for people to come up and and enjoy the weekend and go snowmobiling, go skiing, cross country skiing, and that kind of thing. And, and, and visit their establishments there. Okay. Let let's shift back to Boise and, uh, trains and this I think this will play together on government. Uh, on some of your experience in working with government on projects. Uh, first of all, what was your earliest memory of trains? Well, um, I used to go spend a couple weeks with my grandmother in White Rock, British Columbia, and her little cabin house was right on the road, and be next to the road was the railroad. And so and beyond that was the beach. So there was a every day I'd want to go down and explore. The beaches are fun. You find I, I collected bottle caps for a long time. Yeah. Um, so but every so often a train would come through and, you know, roar through. They would stop at White Rock. But the depot was a ways down the track from my grandmother's house. Not that far, but. So I think my first experience of the the rush of the train coming through the noise, uh, was that and, uh. Was it ever was that a sound at night as well during the day or. Um, it was it was on a schedule. So there would have been some day and some evening, uh, trains coming through. Yeah, yeah. Well, then you mentioned, of course, when you got to Boise in 69 to 70, you were living in River Street. You were doing some work down there. Um, did you remember hearing any train sounds when you were in the River Street neighborhood in Boise? We didn't want to hear them, but we did. Oh. So the switching yard was just, uh, north of our house in River Street. And, uh, so they would switch the the train, uh, cars in the, in the night. And most of the time when they hitched, they would make a huge boom. Oh, yeah. To almost wake you up. Coupling. Yeah. Yeah. Coupling. You got you got used to that. So after a while, you didn't think much of it. And there. Were. There were, there were crossing guards, but crossing uh. Signals to stop you to get across the tracks. Those disappeared after a while. But, uh, for a long time the the the railyard existed there and was part of the the River Street neighborhood, but it wasn't associated with the people at all. It was just a, you know, one of the activities. So so what was how would you describe the, uh, the city development at that time across the tracks in downtown Boise? I would say that it was the city was doing its own thing. They pretty much ignored the River Street. It wasn't till later to a community action agency where I worked, um, and others that started to realize that this, this was a neighborhood that was in need of help. And we had the poverty programs of Lyndon Johnson, I believe, coming through. Um, and so that's when we spend some time today, it's easy to talk to mayor and council about River Street, but at those times there wasn't much connection between poor people and the operation of business and government in downtown. Which was a part of what the city of Boise going through downtown at that time. Something about urban renewal. Well, that's a whole nother chapter of really unfortunate period of time when urban renewal. Sounds like renewal, but it was actually urban removal. Urban destruction? Yeah. So we lost a basic Boise, had a Chinatown at one time on Front Street, front and Capitol there. Um, we lost some of that. Important. There were two story buildings. There were Chinese, there was Chinese cooks. There was a number of small restaurants that were basically Chinese. Uh, was kind of a rich, uh opportunity for the citizens who knew what to do and were willing to try those out, but there wasn't much intermingling of the communities and people at that time that I'm aware of. Um, people kept to themselves, but, uh, you know, um, Chinese people who were there had very minimal income. They would often live in one room with many people in bunks and things like that. And so, um, uh, unfortunately, urban renewal, um, they got ahead of themselves and thought that, that just clearing this land without any they had this vision of building a huge department store complex in the downtown. Uh, and it really we're lucky it didn't happen because imagine downtown Boise trapped now in a big mall, if, you know, uh, luckily, the mall was built out to the west, and we've seen it go from being quite busy to now being rather pedestrian. I mean, it's still it doesn't have the the quality of the shops that it has. It's still busy at Christmas time and things like that. But that was what the urban renewal, um, people thought was going to this was this was across the country. This was a misguided idea across the country of how to stimulate growth in communities. And so, um, we went through rather stagnant period where about four blocks of the downtown was demolished and nothing happened. And you had a huge, big parking lot, essentially. Um, so it was not something that was generating, uh, progress for the downtown. It was stagnant. And, uh, I would go to the BRA Boise Redevelopment Agency meetings. They didn't particularly want me to be there, but it was these were open meetings, and it was my job to keep an eye on what was going on, and I would occasionally get a chance to speak up. And I think that's maybe went over a number of years they finally got some sense. Well, maybe we better do try to do something to help the River Street neighborhood. And so later in that period of time, uh, there were some funds to help do some renovation. Um, Mr. Hoagland at the city of Boise. Um, they started to, uh, do come in and help people. And mainly it was bathrooms were atrocious, you know, um, bad toilets, bad sinks, uh, leaking water, uh, kitchen sinks. Um, so they would come in and make repairs to the bathroom area, kitchen area, do some, maybe paint up, clean up and put a small little house back into some sort of a functioning. Uh, working. Order living quarters. Right? Yeah. And so but that was a slow go. They would make maybe 20 or 30 of those houses, got some of that kind of repair. And I think that was pretty much a a donation. I don't think the, the folks. And again, the issue was why would you fix up a rental of someone else's? This would be the few people that owned a home. Sure, sure. And that would get some of that help. If it was a rental, there'd be "Why would we put money into a rental" that, uh, you know, why should why don't you fix it up? You know, and they were going. Well, I don't know. So. So there was a slow process there, uh, of, uh, of repairs, but some got, some got done and it started a trend, uh, of doing a few houses, um, the house, uh, um, on the corner of 14th and River. Uh, that's a good example where, uh, Betsy Stewart, uh, very lovely lady who was the owner there. And Virgil was her husband. Um, and these people were all getting up in age, too. So, um, so, uh, those are some of the things that that took place and, uh, some it kind of turned the corner there, people. And then I think people started to realize that, well, shoot, River Street is right next to the river, and, uh. And everybody loves the river. Well, yeah, but there really wasn't much. We had a gravel, uh, at the end of, uh, 14th was a was a gravel pit, uh, so, you know, so what was what the users were, were, were, were more industrial. Um, so it wasn't like going down. Um, but then the city started realizing, I mean, this I'm jumping ahead, but city realized the value of the I guess it was the greenbelt when the, um, the Greenbelt plan came along. That was really a very smart thing to do. Um, Bill Anweiler, I think is his name was kind of on the city council. And so they did a they hired an outside consultant. I have a plan somewhere. Greenbelt plan. And that basically showed pathways along the river. And the city gradually started to buy some of that, like that concrete company was owned that property right on the river, you know, and it was all concrete, uh, blade of grass around. Um, so they gradually, lot by lot, started to clean those up and purchase some of those now. And yeah, today it's like a lovely park setting, right? Yeah. It's beautiful along the river. Beautiful. And then we have some other homes like the, uh, Erma Hayman. Right, that are refurbished now and memorialized essentially and uh, viewable as, uh, a memorial. Yeah. Erma was a friend. She was involved in all of our. I might have mentioned this to you the other day. Uh, we rented a bus and we went around. Well, you know, because people we'd have meetings every month at the River Street Design Center. Um, and, uh, they were like, well, let's go look at what's available out there. So we saw some stick homes. We saw some variety of homes. None of them really were in our price range, but it gave everybody, I think, a little bit of a hope of owning a home at some time. And, uh, um, it was just a process of, of, of doing, doing things, you know, so uh, uh, a few homes were, were, were fixed up and, um, the city had did the, the, the, uh, the river, um, plan, uh, and that started to make some small parks along the, you know, start to green up that and minimize the industrial activity and the dumping of crap in the river that. Yeah. I mean, the whole area along River Street, uh, over towards eighth. And it was actually kind of a dumping area in that area for years. Um, if you grew up here, you might remember, you know, that area down there was kind of a quagmire of crap. Yeah. Yeah. Well, I want to make sure I get into this, uh, project that you worked on. That was in another direction from River Street. It was south of River, up on the bench, and it had this beautiful Spanish mission architecture building on top of it. Otherwise known as the Union Pacific Depot, built in 1924 and opened in 1925 as a passenger train depot and service outlet for Boise. Different from what you described near River Street as a switching yard. Another rail activity in Boise, but of a different nature. Uh, what did you think of the the Boise depot up on the bench when you first saw it? Oh, I think I think I thought it was magnificent because it's, uh, it's on a radial alignment with the capital, for one thing. So it's centered right on the major street of the city. Yeah. You can't. It's a beautiful, uh, building. Uh, and I think most people appreciate it, but it started to have fallen into disuse. And, uh, there was issues with the plaster, issues with the restrooms, etcetera, from age. Yeah. And so, um, I had got a pretty close relationship with Mayor Kempthorne. Uh, I was on some committees, and, uh, Mayor Kempthorne, uh, encouraged me to get involved in the, in the in the depot project. And one of the things that happened was not too far after that was that Morrison Knudsen company, which was doing well at that time, decided to purchase the Boise Depot. And this and this was just for me to have this time frame in mind. You came to Boise and 69, the Union Pacific was still operating their passenger train service, Denver to Portland, etc., using that terminal in 70. But 71 1971 was when the last Portland Rose left that terminal and and shut down essentially train operations. Then in 77, six years later, Amtrak service was regenerated over that same track and the Union Pacific still owned the station, but rented it to Amtrak under the nationwide contract that Richard Nixon signed with Amtrak a dollar a month for use of that that station. Amtrak, opened up in 77, and then Morrison Knudsen came along and in 1990 purchased that property and building after Amtrak had been running for, you know, roughly ten years there. Correct. And that's when obviously, a number of people were seeing that Union Pacific had left it in 71 when the service stopped in somewhat tawdry condition. Union Pacific didn't improve it any while Amtrak was there. Amtrak couldn't afford to improve it, but was gracious to have something to use for a terminal, and MK came in to buy it. And that's the project you're talking about. That Mayor Kempthorne said you ought to get involved in this. So how did that work out that you got involved? Well, I want to mention I did get to take Amtrak over to Portland. Yeah. Uh, at one time. And, uh, it's a beautiful route. I wish it was still available today. Um, very scenic. And end up spending the night in a hotel and then took the train back. Uh, so that was and I it was clickety, clickety clack, you know, um. Good service. Uh, ran. Well, so I had. Great views, great. Fond memories of that. And I, uh, um, so, um, uh, because of my involvement with the with Mayor Kempthorne, he introduced me to the people of Morrison Knudsen. Okay. And that's how I got involved with them. And, uh, I was more their local. They brought in they brought in a group, uh, George Notter and Associates out of Boston. Right. So they hired a very astute, uh planning firm and preservation, preservation and restoration. Yeah. Right. Right. Done a lot of work, and, uh, I became their kind of local, uh, contact. I would pick them up at the airport. I would kind of give them some information, help them get around a little bit first. They adapted pretty easy. And, uh, so I was part of that team, so I was lucky to be in on the ground level of that new team that was looking at how to restore the depot for Morrison Knudsen. And it took, as I recall, after the purchase at the end of 90. It took a year for MK as a general contractor to bring in specialists and do some of their own engineering work on what do we need to do, make plans on what they're going to need to do. And then it was 91 when, uh, probably Notter and Associates as well as some of the MK subs had a plan to follow. Yeah. Okay. Yeah. And then in that plan, especially, uh, in your area of, of, uh, interest preservation, uh, Notter had to deal with things that needed to be upgraded, I'm sure. Certainly. Code city code had changed from 1924 to 1990. Um, and then there were repairs that needed to be made. Uh, did you see any challenges that the Notter team was having in, uh, being able to get approval essentially to institute changes that didn't change the historical value. It was it was on the National Register of Historical Places when they bought it. Well, I think that the, the, the movement was forward. You know, everybody wanted to see see it done. People appreciated the, uh, the depot and its alignment to the capital and its location next to two great parks Ann Morrison, Julia Davis, just down the way. Um, so, uh, uh, that team got into the details. I think it was, uh, you know, what do we do with certain pieces? We knew that it needed some work in the interior. They ended up repainting some of the interior pieces. I, uh, I saw they rented a highboy. They were very good at it. They were very Notter and his group were very thorough in their evaluation. They rented a highboy which would allow them to get up to the top into the timbers. Yeah, inside and look it. And there was, there was one rafter that was, uh, had come somewhat apart, wasn't, wasn't, uh, squared up like it should be. So that wasn't something that needed to be done, you know now. So basically they would make note of that, draw up some plans, put that in as a work order for for MK or whatever to start looking at how they might do that. Yeah. And that was part of my job was to, as I say, kind of assist where needed. Uh, I wasn't doing the repairs, sure, but I was there. I was there more to help Notter and Associates, um, navigate, get to and from, uh, connect them with things and lucky to have that opportunity as a young man. Did you see any evidence of, uh. There being problems or difficulty in certain kinds of materials to replace what was there to, to make it original or to make it more like it was original, to get the paints, to get the wood or whatever? Some of the metal parts. Um, that was a problem because it was hard to get those materials that were built when it was from being 1920s. Yeah. For to, you know, to the 80s or 70s. Yeah. Yeah. And so, uh, uh, that was a, that was I don't, I think more we were there, we weren't tearing stuff down and replacing it. We were repairing things. So I mean, it was the areas that they concentrated on were basically the tower. And, you know, it was very unusual to decide to build an elevator in an elevator. Yeah, it was a spiral staircase, a very narrow, thin spiral case going round and round. I actually have a 16ft of it that I asked if I could have. And they gave. Oh. Lucky you. Yeah. So, um. Uh, but, uh, it was, as I say, repairs. And, uh, the biggest, one of the biggest problems was they never could quite get a handle on the exterior plaster of the depot, and that failed a number of times. The the other things were, you know, they had those beautiful benches in the depot and, uh, so they the depot had never been really, uh, um, uh, uh, let, let go. It always had had maintenance and care, but, uh, it was still I mean, those benches were and uh, a couple of those got moved out of there and moved, I think one went to MK, one went out to the Idaho Transportation Department. Some of those benches, uh, disappeared. Um, but, uh, um, the Barkalow Newsstand was always something that we all understood was something that shouldn't. Don't touch that. That's going to stay exactly the way it is. Uh, let's figure out a way to use that. Put it back into a way. Now, it's pretty much a display area for the depot. But at the time, we, you know, we never quite sure if we'd get trains back again or what would make, you know, it didn't look great, but you never knew that. But I guess I really appreciated the thoroughness of the of the team. Uh, yeah, they were team to. Come. In and be professional and to to to, uh, to figure out what was, what should be done. It seemed to me in the depot, uh, passenger lounge or waiting room. It seemed like, um, compared to all of the other restoration that needed to be done. Like you're saying, the the stucco on the outside had to be fixed. The roof tiles, half the roof tiles had to be replaced, you know, all of that. And then some of the, you know, underground stuff. It seemed like the engineers, once they got inside the passenger depot, they looked around and said, well, I don't think we have to do anything in here, so to speak, because it's all still original tile on the floors. Uh, a lot of the wood around sills and such are still original. Um, and the and the windows that go up, you know, two, two levels upstairs. A lot of the features that are up in the tower were taken out, but the tower itself, you know, is two tiers of bricks that go up 100ft, and it's all still original. But there was a there was this one area above the Barkalow stand, um, that it appeared people thought this could be repurposed, and they built that stairwell stairway up over the Barkalow sign and, uh, and had a room up there. Now, the original room actually was storage from the plans and from the Barkalow Brothers operation notes. It was like storage for the Barkalow Brothers, uh, supplies. But it seems now like it's a coveted area that on the west side, up over the stand. um, was remodeled. And today the city owning it uses it as a wedding feature with the stairwell, and they call it the Bridal Changing room, which can dramatize the entrance of the bride or whatever to the event, you know, on the stairwell and all that kind of stuff. But I figure Morrison Knudsen probably had a little bit different idea when they came in and bought it and restored it for what they wanted to use it for. Do you remember what? Well, um, yeah, it became, um, they filled it up with, uh, beautiful furniture from Hendren's. Uh, it should have been Arts and Crafts furniture, but very plushy, uh, couches and chairs in that room for a period of time. And this was part of MK's plan. If you had visitors coming into the depot that they brought in from around the world. Yeah. Business visitors was to bring people and take them up to the depot. And as you mentioned, the depot's interior has always been in pretty good shape. You know, um, one of the things I remember a point I'm getting off subject, but, uh, was when they I saw they brought a crane in and they took the lid, so to speak, off the top of parts of the depot. Yeah. The top roof. Right. Off the tower. All came, came up. And I think that's how they got the elevator in there, too. Exactly. Yeah. So, uh, there were some major changes in that piece. They're not changes, but, uh, temporary openings and closure into that. But the, uh, the bridal room, um, was was the special. It wasn't called that when the MK had it, but it was it was that was the special area that to bring a dignitary, you'd probably walk through the depot. Let's go up to the, uh, our, our room above and let's talk about a couple deals. We want a conference room or whatever. Yeah, exactly. And I think that's that was hopefully that maybe to complete a deal here and there on a dam or a or whatever. Yeah. Yeah, exactly. So, um. That, uh, I've, I've met, uh, a number of Union Pacific employees, former employees. The years I've been here in Boise and, uh, a number of them, when I would ask them about the depot and their thoughts about it and stuff, a number of them just immediately made a reference to, oh, the Million Dollar view building. And I think it was because that that second floor office or meeting room up there had that gorgeous view of the front range out to the north and to the west, the parks and the the valley that the Boise's in, heading out to the west and to the south, off to the, Owyhee mountains, you know, and they called it the Million Dollar View Room. So do you think that you think that MK restoration, which was that 1990 to 93 period before they had it operational? Um, was a good job? I think, yeah, they did it really well. I mean, it was bringing in Notter, you know, they they didn't bring in local guy. They brought in the national team to, to uh, to do this. So they had excellent advice. Uh, they had the resources to do it at that time. MK was still was still going. Um, and so the idea was, let's do it first class. As we end this interview. I want to ask you, would you like to see train service back in Boise again? Of course. I told you I once took the train over to Portland and I live. Fortunately, I bought a house 40 years ago right across from the depot. I look at the depot every day. Your neighbor, your neighbor right there. And I see a lot of it. And, uh, so, um, I would I, I have a couple of thoughts here. I think there might be a short term solution. I, I hope I'm almost 80 years old. Uh, that in my lifetime I'll be able to see the depot working again. But I worry that that's, uh, closing in on me a little bit. Um, one thought I have. This is a would be a light rail system from, uh, Nampa Caldwell, Boise to Morrison Knudsen. The reason I've got. Or. To I mean, uh, the the the tech um, Micron. Micron Industrial. Micron. Right. Yeah. Yeah. So, um, there's enough energy and and money out in micron to help make something like that happen. And you'd be able to get people who want to go to work from Nampa Caldwell Boise out to Micron back and forth. So that could be a light rail. That seems like a short term solution that might allow that to happen in not too many years. I mean, you could there's enough there's a track almost all the way. You'd have to have build some track to get to Micron up there. But, uh, seems like that would make sense because if you've gone out there recently, there's so many people working in that area. Oh, heavens. Yes. It's it's a very, very congested, busy area. Yeah. And imagine and if you've been traveling our freeways, you should be able to move pretty quickly around. Not necessarily now. Rush hours you get freeways, get backed up. So it could really. Plus the cost of housing today with Boise having grown so much and will be growing more with that development. The cost of housing in the local Boise area around a place like Micron is going sky high, and it's it's more cost effective to be, at least with starting homes, maybe even mid-range homes with kids to to buy or build out to the west of Boise, which just pushes more. Yeah, doesn't doesn't create any. More out there. And that'll add more more to the interstate for. That's what I mean. That's why it seemed like a short term system that connect these places and get the system going through a light rail, and then hopes of somehow getting the, you know, the national scale back up. Going over to Portland and down to Salt Lake could come later. I mean, I would love to see, I just don't I just worry that time is slipping by all the time. And we're not getting any getting anywhere. Yeah. So some sort of a short term solution? Light rail for a while would be helpful in that. In that smaller distance. Well, I'm sorry to see that our time is slipping away as well on this interview. We've touched on a lot of your experience and thoughts about Boise's history and your involvement in it, which is great, and I'm sure there could be more interviews, that we could have more interview topics. But I want to bring this to an end today. And thank you very much for allowing us in your home there to, uh, hear, uh, of your stories and, uh, I hope to get back to you again and maybe, uh, chart out another interview topic or two. Thank you. It's been a pleasure. Thank you. Okay.

lines.line: # Introduction and Purpose of Interview

> The interviewer, Thad Webster, introduces the America 250 Idaho Oral History Project, outlining its mission to commemorate Idaho's role in America's legacy and advance the ideals of the American Revolution. He explains the context and goals of the interview with John Bertram, a city planner, and sets expectations for the topics to be covered, including Boise's historical preservation and train history.

metadata.title: Introduction and Purpose of Interview
metadata.synopsis: The interviewer, Thad Webster, introduces the America 250 Idaho Oral History Project, outlining its mission to commemorate Idaho's role in America's legacy and advance the ideals of the American Revolution. He explains the context and goals of the interview with John Bertram, a city planner, and sets expectations for the topics to be covered, including Boise's historical preservation and train history.

lines.line: # John Bertram's Background and Path to City Planning

> John Bertram shares how he was influenced by his father's academic connections and a professor at the University of Washington to pursue city planning. He recounts joining Vista Volunteers in Service to America in 1969, which led him to Boise unexpectedly. His initial intention was to serve in eastern cities, but he was assigned to Idaho, where he settled in the River Street neighborhood and began his journey in community service and urban planning.

metadata.title: John Bertram's Background and Path to City Planning
metadata.synopsis: John Bertram shares how he was influenced by his father's academic connections and a professor at the University of Washington to pursue city planning. He recounts joining Vista Volunteers in Service to America in 1969, which led him to Boise unexpectedly. His initial intention was to serve in eastern cities, but he was assigned to Idaho, where he settled in the River Street neighborhood and began his journey in community service and urban planning.

lines.line: # Early Community Projects in River Street Neighborhood

> John describes his early efforts in the River Street neighborhood, focusing on house painting projects to improve living conditions for low-income residents. He highlights the generosity of local businesses, the recruitment of Boise State students for manpower, and the challenges of working in a predominantly renter-occupied, mixed-race community. These projects laid the foundation for his engagement in neighborhood improvement and community building.

metadata.title: Early Community Projects in River Street Neighborhood
metadata.synopsis: John describes his early efforts in the River Street neighborhood, focusing on house painting projects to improve living conditions for low-income residents. He highlights the generosity of local businesses, the recruitment of Boise State students for manpower, and the challenges of working in a predominantly renter-occupied, mixed-race community. These projects laid the foundation for his engagement in neighborhood improvement and community building.

lines.line: # Transition from Vista to Homeownership and Skills

> After his Vista service, John purchased a house in River Street for under $10,000 and began extensive repairs, drawing on skills learned from his father. He recounts personal experiences with home maintenance, including dealing with structural issues and the lack of prior upkeep. His hands-on approach and family background in repairs helped him restore the property, which remains in his family to this day.

metadata.title: Transition from Vista to Homeownership and Skills
metadata.synopsis: After his Vista service, John purchased a house in River Street for under $10,000 and began extensive repairs, drawing on skills learned from his father. He recounts personal experiences with home maintenance, including dealing with structural issues and the lack of prior upkeep. His hands-on approach and family background in repairs helped him restore the property, which remains in his family to this day.

lines.line: # Early Urban Planning Work and Historic Preservation

> John discusses founding his firm, Plan Makers, and his initial urban planning projects in Boise. He describes working with property owners like Winston Moore and Joan Carley in historic districts such as Old Boise and Hyde Park. His work involved creating plans, mapping boundaries, cataloging buildings, and encouraging preservation efforts, often drawing on research from the Idaho State Historical Society archives.

metadata.title: Early Urban Planning Work and Historic Preservation
metadata.synopsis: John discusses founding his firm, Plan Makers, and his initial urban planning projects in Boise. He describes working with property owners like Winston Moore and Joan Carley in historic districts such as Old Boise and Hyde Park. His work involved creating plans, mapping boundaries, cataloging buildings, and encouraging preservation efforts, often drawing on research from the Idaho State Historical Society archives.

lines.line: # Idaho City Preservation Projects and Grant Writing

> John details his involvement in Idaho City, focusing on its mining history and the challenges of preserving historic boardwalks and buildings. He emphasizes the importance of community engagement, encouraging property owners to participate in restoration, and his success in securing grants for city-owned properties like the old church turned City Hall. These efforts aimed to make Idaho City more attractive for tourism and local recreation.

metadata.title: Idaho City Preservation Projects and Grant Writing
metadata.synopsis: John details his involvement in Idaho City, focusing on its mining history and the challenges of preserving historic boardwalks and buildings. He emphasizes the importance of community engagement, encouraging property owners to participate in restoration, and his success in securing grants for city-owned properties like the old church turned City Hall. These efforts aimed to make Idaho City more attractive for tourism and local recreation.

lines.line: # Collaboration with City Groups and Community Leaders

> John reflects on his interactions with city councils, influential community members, and leaders such as Byron Johnson and Ron Terwilliger. He describes the process of securing funding, writing grants, and fostering collective action among property owners to advance historic preservation. His dedication and prior successes earned him respect and support from local government and community stakeholders.

metadata.title: Collaboration with City Groups and Community Leaders
metadata.synopsis: John reflects on his interactions with city councils, influential community members, and leaders such as Byron Johnson and Ron Terwilliger. He describes the process of securing funding, writing grants, and fostering collective action among property owners to advance historic preservation. His dedication and prior successes earned him respect and support from local government and community stakeholders.

lines.line: # Boise's Train History and Early Memories

> The interviewer asks John about his earliest memories of trains, which he traces back to childhood visits in White Rock, British Columbia, where trains passed near his grandmother's house. He recalls the sensory experience of trains and later connects these memories to his time living in Boise's River Street neighborhood, where the sounds of the nearby switching yard became a regular part of life.

metadata.title: Boise's Train History and Early Memories
metadata.synopsis: The interviewer asks John about his earliest memories of trains, which he traces back to childhood visits in White Rock, British Columbia, where trains passed near his grandmother's house. He recalls the sensory experience of trains and later connects these memories to his time living in Boise's River Street neighborhood, where the sounds of the nearby switching yard became a regular part of life.

lines.line: # River Street, Rail Yards, and City Development

> John describes the disconnect between the River Street neighborhood and downtown Boise during the late 1960s and early 1970s. He notes that the city largely ignored River Street, which was characterized by poverty and limited interaction with business and government. The rail yard was a significant presence but operated independently of the neighborhood's residents, highlighting broader issues of urban segregation and neglect.

metadata.title: River Street, Rail Yards, and City Development
metadata.synopsis: John describes the disconnect between the River Street neighborhood and downtown Boise during the late 1960s and early 1970s. He notes that the city largely ignored River Street, which was characterized by poverty and limited interaction with business and government. The rail yard was a significant presence but operated independently of the neighborhood's residents, highlighting broader issues of urban segregation and neglect.

lines.line: # Urban Renewal and Its Impact on Boise

> John discusses the era of urban renewal in Boise, describing it as 'urban removal' due to the destruction of historic areas like Chinatown. He criticizes the misguided national trend of clearing land for large-scale development, which led to stagnation and the loss of cultural heritage. He recounts attending Boise Redevelopment Agency meetings and the eventual shift toward recognizing and assisting neighborhoods like River Street.

metadata.title: Urban Renewal and Its Impact on Boise
metadata.synopsis: John discusses the era of urban renewal in Boise, describing it as 'urban removal' due to the destruction of historic areas like Chinatown. He criticizes the misguided national trend of clearing land for large-scale development, which led to stagnation and the loss of cultural heritage. He recounts attending Boise Redevelopment Agency meetings and the eventual shift toward recognizing and assisting neighborhoods like River Street.

lines.line: # Emergence of the Greenbelt and Riverfront Parks

> John recounts the transformation of the River Street area through the development of the Boise Greenbelt. He credits city leaders and consultants for envisioning pathways and parks along the river, which led to the acquisition and cleanup of industrial properties. The area evolved from a dumping ground to a scenic and beloved park setting, enhancing the city's natural beauty and recreational opportunities.

metadata.title: Emergence of the Greenbelt and Riverfront Parks
metadata.synopsis: John recounts the transformation of the River Street area through the development of the Boise Greenbelt. He credits city leaders and consultants for envisioning pathways and parks along the river, which led to the acquisition and cleanup of industrial properties. The area evolved from a dumping ground to a scenic and beloved park setting, enhancing the city's natural beauty and recreational opportunities.

lines.line: # Community Engagement and Homeownership Initiatives

> John highlights efforts to promote homeownership and community pride in River Street, including organizing bus tours to explore housing options. He mentions the role of local figures like Erma Harmon in fostering hope and engagement among residents. These initiatives contributed to the gradual improvement of the neighborhood and the preservation of its historical character.

metadata.title: Community Engagement and Homeownership Initiatives
metadata.synopsis: John highlights efforts to promote homeownership and community pride in River Street, including organizing bus tours to explore housing options. He mentions the role of local figures like Erma Harmon in fostering hope and engagement among residents. These initiatives contributed to the gradual improvement of the neighborhood and the preservation of its historical character.

lines.line: # The Boise Depot: Architecture and Historical Significance

> The interviewer introduces the Boise Depot, emphasizing its Spanish mission architecture, historical role as a passenger train terminal, and prominent location aligned with the state capitol. John expresses admiration for the building's design and significance, noting its initial grandeur and subsequent decline due to disuse and aging infrastructure.

metadata.title: The Boise Depot: Architecture and Historical Significance
metadata.synopsis: The interviewer introduces the Boise Depot, emphasizing its Spanish mission architecture, historical role as a passenger train terminal, and prominent location aligned with the state capitol. John expresses admiration for the building's design and significance, noting its initial grandeur and subsequent decline due to disuse and aging infrastructure.

lines.line: # Decline of Train Service and Ownership Changes

> The conversation outlines the timeline of train service at the Boise Depot, from Union Pacific's operations to Amtrak's brief revival and eventual cessation. The depot's ownership transitions from Union Pacific to Morrison-Knudsen, which purchased the property in 1990 after years of neglect. The discussion sets the stage for the restoration efforts that followed.

metadata.title: Decline of Train Service and Ownership Changes
metadata.synopsis: The conversation outlines the timeline of train service at the Boise Depot, from Union Pacific's operations to Amtrak's brief revival and eventual cessation. The depot's ownership transitions from Union Pacific to Morrison-Knudsen, which purchased the property in 1990 after years of neglect. The discussion sets the stage for the restoration efforts that followed.

lines.line: # Restoration of the Boise Depot: Planning and Challenges

> John describes his involvement in the depot's restoration, facilitated by Mayor Kempthorne and Morrison-Knudsen. He served as a local liaison for George Nader & Associates, a Boston-based preservation firm. The restoration faced challenges related to updating the building to modern codes, sourcing period-appropriate materials, and addressing structural issues, all while maintaining historical integrity.

metadata.title: Restoration of the Boise Depot: Planning and Challenges
metadata.synopsis: John describes his involvement in the depot's restoration, facilitated by Mayor Kempthorne and Morrison-Knudsen. He served as a local liaison for George Nader & Associates, a Boston-based preservation firm. The restoration faced challenges related to updating the building to modern codes, sourcing period-appropriate materials, and addressing structural issues, all while maintaining historical integrity.

lines.line: # Restoration Details: Interior, Tower, and Special Features

> The discussion delves into specific aspects of the depot's restoration, including the preservation of original tile, woodwork, and windows in the passenger lounge. John describes the installation of an elevator in the tower, the handling of historic benches, and the careful preservation of the Barklow newsstand. He notes the creation of a special room above the newsstand, which was later repurposed for events and weddings.

metadata.title: Restoration Details: Interior, Tower, and Special Features
metadata.synopsis: The discussion delves into specific aspects of the depot's restoration, including the preservation of original tile, woodwork, and windows in the passenger lounge. John describes the installation of an elevator in the tower, the handling of historic benches, and the careful preservation of the Barklow newsstand. He notes the creation of a special room above the newsstand, which was later repurposed for events and weddings.

lines.line: # The Depot's Role for Morrison-Knudsen and Community

> John explains how Morrison-Knudsen furnished the depot with elegant furniture to host business visitors and dignitaries. The special room above the newsstand served as a conference space with a 'Million Dollar View' of Boise's landscape. He recalls significant restoration activities, such as removing the tower roof for elevator installation, and reflects on the depot's continued prominence and utility.

metadata.title: The Depot's Role for Morrison-Knudsen and Community
metadata.synopsis: John explains how Morrison-Knudsen furnished the depot with elegant furniture to host business visitors and dignitaries. The special room above the newsstand served as a conference space with a 'Million Dollar View' of Boise's landscape. He recalls significant restoration activities, such as removing the tower roof for elevator installation, and reflects on the depot's continued prominence and utility.

lines.line: # Reflections on Train Service and Future Prospects

> John expresses his desire to see train service return to Boise, sharing personal memories of living near the depot and riding Amtrak. He proposes a light rail system connecting Nampa, Caldwell, Boise, and Micron as a practical short-term solution to address transportation and housing challenges. He reflects on Boise's growth, the need for improved transit, and his hope to witness the depot's revival within his lifetime.

He reflects on Boise's growth, the need for improved transit, and his hope to witness the depot's revival within his lifetime.

story.title: John Bertram: City of Boise historical preservation
story.description: This interview covers a lifetime of the Narrator's professional and personal efforts and work to help identify important City areas of need, make improvement plans and highlight related historical facts.
